course_title: Postcolonial and Decolonial Thought and Politics

learning_outcomes.description: Students will have developed an advanced level understanding of post- and decolonial thought and politics.
An ability to successfully analyse the challenges and opportunities that post- and decolonial thought and politics bring to bear on dominant approaches to world order.
Will be able to successfully analyse case examples, including their specific and broader social and political implications.
Be able to identify and evaluate (international) institutional arrangements and responses to challenges of post- and decolonial social and political relations.
Will gain an appreciation to cultural diversity and associated political possibilities.

assessments.task_description:
Task Description: Reflections (20%; 800-1000 words).

           The Reflection Assignment for Postcolonial and Decolonial Thought and Politics is to give you the opportunity to consider how your understanding of world politics is changing as a consequence of the learning experiences in the course thus far.

           Think about how and why the seminars and readings provided you with new or surprising insights, or whether issues, materials and literatures discussed rather confirmed or consolidated things you already knew or perhaps expected.

           As you do this, make sure that you focus on what was particularly important for you. Rather than provide lengthy, generic summaries for what was raised in the seminars, you should concentrate on recording what was particularly important, intriguing, or challenging from your perspective. Identifying questions that the sessions, readings or discussions have raised for you is an excellent way to conclude your reflection, and to help you achieve the ‘learning-feedback-loop” we are aiming for: Doing this exercise by producing a well-written and coherent text will leave you with a document that helps you recall and process the learning experiences from the course.

           For this type of assignment, the usual academic conventions regarding citation-practices and bibliographic details apply. Texts, evidence, or other bits of information that you integrate into your reflection, if you need them, will have to be referenced accordingly. If you are unsure about how do this, please consult the POLSIS Essay Writing Guide (POLSIS Essay Guide 2026.pdf). Please note, though, that you are not writing an essay for this assignment; reflections are more about your personal learning experience, offering an opportunity for you to document your learning process, and to receive feedback on that.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments for this course (Reflection) will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
You will write a research essay of 2500 words (excluding reference list) on a topic you can choose from a list of questions provided during the early weeks of the course.

Prepare and submit your research essay (via Turnitin) by the specified due date.

Please note: You should have a full draft of your essay ready two weeks before submission to give yourself time to reflect on and improve your submission.

The essay needs to clearly state the essay question up front. The layout should use double-spacing. The word-count needs to be provided at the end of the essay's text, before the reference list. You are required to engage with the mandatory and recommended readings from the course reading list as relevant for your question in addition to any other sources you may wish to consult.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments for this course (Research Essay) will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
You will need to select and answer two questions from a list of six by writing discursive analytical essays. Ensure that your response is well-structured, logical, and presents your arguments in a progressive and integrated manner.

Your exam and the available questions enable you to synthesize the knowledge you have gained throughout the course. Please make sure to address the chosen question(s) explicitly, clearly, and comprehensively. Since this is a 'closed book' exam, where you reference materials from memory, we do not expect your information to be comprehensive or fully accurate (for example, 'verbatim citations' or the correct year of publication).

Be sure to write legibly and organize your arguments before you begin writing. Take the time to plan your paragraphs and build a logical progression in your essay.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Welcome and Introduction

In our first session, we will take a close look at the course-program, including an outline of the themes and topics covered. We will also discuss the two required readings by way of opening the course substantively.
learning_activities.topic_description: Welcome and Introduction In our first session, we will take a close look at the course-program, including an outline of the themes and topics covered. We will also discuss the two required readings by way of opening the course substantively.

learning_activities.topic:
The conquest of the Americas

In this session, we introduce and examine the example of the European colonisation of the Caribbean and the Americas. This is a crucial period in the history of colonialism and subsequent struggles for liberation.
learning_activities.topic_description: The conquest of the Americas In this session, we introduce and examine the example of the European colonisation of the Caribbean and the Americas. This is a crucial period in the history of colonialism and subsequent struggles for liberation.

learning_activities.topic:
Colonialism in Global Relations

Today we focus on how colonialism has shaped global social and political relations across five continents. Among other things, we will consider the extent to which contemporary social and political challenges are rooted in, or refracted through these relations.
learning_activities.topic_description: Colonialism in Global Relations Today we focus on how colonialism has shaped global social and political relations across five continents. Among other things, we will consider the extent to which contemporary social and political challenges are rooted in, or refracted through these relations.

learning_activities.topic:
Colonial representations, rule and extraction

In this session, we will examine justifications of colonial rule and the concrete implications manifest in exploitation, discrimination and domination, engendering resistance. One example will be the conception and application of the ‘dual mandate’, a system of colonial rule developed in the context of the British Empire.
learning_activities.topic_description: Colonial representations, rule and extraction In this session, we will examine justifications of colonial rule and the concrete implications manifest in exploitation, discrimination and domination, engendering resistance. One example will be the conception and application of the ‘dual mandate’, a system of colonial rule developed in the context of the British Empire.

learning_activities.topic:
Colonial Political Economies: Enslavement and Plantations

We focus on the political economy of colonisation, and in particular the significance and impact of the widespread imposition of plantations. In this context, we address the horrendous implications of enslavement and the trade in enslaved peoples.
learning_activities.topic_description: Colonial Political Economies: Enslavement and Plantations We focus on the political economy of colonisation, and in particular the significance and impact of the widespread imposition of plantations. In this context, we address the horrendous implications of enslavement and the trade in enslaved peoples.

learning_activities.topic:
Liberation struggles – Quilombos

Resistance to colonisation sometimes took the form of founding communities away from the reach of colonisers. These provided both a refuge from enslavement and oppression, and bases for organising political resistance from the vantage point of an alternative political project.
learning_activities.topic_description: Liberation struggles – Quilombos Resistance to colonisation sometimes took the form of founding communities away from the reach of colonisers. These provided both a refuge from enslavement and oppression, and bases for organising political resistance from the vantage point of an alternative political project.

learning_activities.topic:
Colonial Corporations

Today we examine colonial corporations and their role in driving and shaping colonisation. We will look at different examples of how such corporations operated, and what their impact was (or has been!) in colonised contexts.
learning_activities.topic_description: Colonial Corporations Today we examine colonial corporations and their role in driving and shaping colonisation. We will look at different examples of how such corporations operated, and what their impact was (or has been!) in colonised contexts.

learning_activities.topic:
Anti-colonial struggles and the decolonisation project

Anti-colonial struggles and decolonisation have marked a significant shift in global social and political power relations. At the same time, the postcolonial context has remained inflected by colonial logics, and we will explore this in more detail.
learning_activities.topic_description: Anti-colonial struggles and the decolonisation project Anti-colonial struggles and decolonisation have marked a significant shift in global social and political power relations. At the same time, the postcolonial context has remained inflected by colonial logics, and we will explore this in more detail.

learning_activities.topic:
Politics of the post-1945 international development project

The post-1945 international development project has been framed and operationalised in terms of hierarchy in world politics. We look at its underlying assumptions, and social, economic, and political implications in light of colonial logics and legacies.
learning_activities.topic_title: Politics of the post-1945 international development project
learning_activities.topic_description: Politics of the post-1945 international development project The post-1945 international development project has been framed and operationalised in terms of hierarchy in world politics. We look at its underlying assumptions, and social, economic, and political implications in light of colonial logics and legacies.

learning_activities.topic:
Settler Colonialism and Indigenous Struggles

Settler colonialism has ongoing implications for anti-colonial and decolonial struggles. Today, we examine and discuss the case of indigenous resistance and revival in the North American context.
learning_activities.topic_description: Settler Colonialism and Indigenous Struggles Settler colonialism has ongoing implications for anti-colonial and decolonial struggles. Today, we examine and discuss the case of indigenous resistance and revival in the North American context.

learning_activities.topic:
Debating Reparations and ‘just repair’

The question of reparations for the harms inflicted by colonisation has been gaining political traction. This week, we examine some of the challenges encountered in this context, including the question of the referent of reparations.
learning_activities.topic_description: Debating Reparations and ‘just repair’ The question of reparations for the harms inflicted by colonisation has been gaining political traction. This week, we examine some of the challenges encountered in this context, including the question of the referent of reparations.

learning_activities.topic:
Wrap Up

In our final session, we retrace the learning experiences together, and discuss further implications of ongoing struggles over colonialism and decolonisation.
learning_activities.topic_description: Wrap Up In our final session, we retrace the learning experiences together, and discuss further implications of ongoing struggles over colonialism and decolonisation.
